name: flask-security-invenio
documentation:
Flask-Security-Invenio
======================

WARNING: This is a private fork of Flask-Security used for Invenio. Please use
`Flask-Security-Too <https://flask-security-too.readthedocs.io/>`_ instead.

documentation:
flask_security.babel
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

I18N support for Flask-Security.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: flask_security.babel.Translations
documentation: Fixes WTForms translation support and uses wtforms translations.

modules.name: flask_security.confirmable
documentation:
flask_security.confirmable
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Flask-Security confirmable module

:copyright: (c) 2012 by Matt Wright.
:copyright: (c) 2017 by CERN.
:license: MIT, see LICENSE for more details.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: flask_security.confirmable.send_confirmation_instructions
documentation:
Sends the confirmation instructions email for the specified user.

```
param user

The user to send the instructions to
```

functions.name: flask_security.confirmable.generate_confirmation_token
documentation:
Generates a unique confirmation token for the specified user.

```
param user

The user to work with
```

functions.name: flask_security.confirmable.requires_confirmation
documentation: Returns `True` if the user requires confirmation.

functions.name: flask_security.confirmable.confirm_email_token_status
documentation:
Returns the expired status, invalid status, and user of a confirmation
token. For example:

```
expired, invalid, user = confirm_email_token_status('...')
```

```
param token

The confirmation token
```

functions.name: flask_security.confirmable.confirm_user
documentation:
Confirms the specified user

```
param user

The user to confirm
```

modules.name: flask_security
documentation:
flask_security
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Flask-Security is a Flask extension that aims to add quick and simple
security via Flask-Login, Flask-Principal, Flask-WTF, and passlib.

:copyright: (c) 2012 by Matt Wright.
:license: MIT, see LICENSE for more details.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: flask_security.recoverable.send_reset_password_instructions
documentation:
Sends the reset password instructions email for the specified user.

```
param user

The user to send the instructions to
```

documentation:
Sends the password reset notice email for the specified user.

```
param user

The user to send the notice to
```

documentation:
Generates a unique reset password token for the specified user.

```

The user to work with

documentation:
Returns the expired status, invalid status, and user of a password reset
token. For example:

```

```

```

The password reset token

documentation:
Update the specified user's password

```

The user to update_password

```

modules.name: flask_security.utils
documentation:
flask_security.utils
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Flask-Security utils module

:copyright: (c) 2012 by Matt Wright.
:license: MIT, see LICENSE for more details.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: flask_security.utils._
documentation: Identity function to mark strings for translation.

functions.name: flask_security.utils.impersonate_user
documentation:
Impersonate a user.

Logouts the current user, and login the new user.

Can login inactive users and doesn't track login time.

functions.name: flask_security.utils.login_user
documentation:
Perform the login routine.

If SECURITY_TRACKABLE is used, make sure you commit changes after this
request (i.e. `app.security.datastore.commit()`).

```
param user

The user to login
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.logout_user
documentation:
Logs out the current.

This will also clean up the remember me cookie if it exists.

functions.name: flask_security.utils.get_hmac
documentation:
Returns a Base64 encoded HMAC+SHA512 of the password signed with
the salt specified by `SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT`.

```
param password

The password to sign
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.verify_password
documentation:
Returns `True` if the password matches the supplied hash.

```
param password

A plaintext password to verify

param password_hash

The expected hash value of the password
(usually from your database)
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.verify_and_update_password
documentation:
Returns `True` if the password is valid for the specified user.

Additionally, the hashed password in the database is updated if the
hashing algorithm happens to have changed.

```
param password

A plaintext password to verify

param user

The user to verify against
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.encrypt_password
documentation:
Encrypt the specified plaintext password.

It uses the configured encryption options.

:::{deprecated} 2.0.2 Use :func:`hash_password` instead.
:::

```
param password

The plaintext password to encrypt
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.hash_password
documentation:
Hash the specified plaintext password.

It uses the configured hashing options.

:::{versionadded} 2.0.2
:::

```
param password

The plaintext password to hash
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.encode_string
documentation:
Encodes a string to bytes, if it isn't already.

```
param string

The string to encode
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.do_flash
documentation:
Flash a message depending on if the `FLASH_MESSAGES` configuration
value is set.

```
param message

The flash message

param category

The flash message category
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.get_url
documentation:
Returns a URL if a valid endpoint is found. Otherwise, returns the
provided value.

```
param endpoint_or_url

The endpoint name or URL to default to
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.slash_url_suffix
documentation:
Adds a slash either to the beginning or the end of a suffix
(which is to be appended to a URL), depending on whether or not
the URL ends with a slash.

functions.name: flask_security.utils.url_for_security
documentation:
Return a URL for the security blueprint

```
param endpoint

the endpoint of the URL (name of the function)

param values

the variable arguments of the URL rule

param _external

if set to , an absolute URL is generated. Server
address can be changed via  configuration variable which
defaults to .

param _anchor

if provided this is added as anchor to the URL.

param _method

if provided this explicitly specifies an HTTP method.
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.find_redirect
documentation:
Returns the URL to redirect to after a user logs in successfully.

```
param key

The session or application configuration key to search for
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.get_config
documentation:
Conveniently get the security configuration for the specified
application without the annoying '[SECURITY]' prefix.

```
param app

The application to inspect
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.config_value
documentation:
Get a Flask-Security configuration value.

```
param key

The configuration key without the prefix 

param app

An optional specific application to inspect. Defaults to
Flask's 

param default

An optional default value if the value is not set
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.get_within_delta
documentation:
Get a timedelta object from the application configuration following
the internal convention of:

```
<Amount of Units> <Type of Units>
```

Examples of valid config values:

```
5 days
10 minutes
```

```
param key

The config value key without the  prefix

param app

Optional application to inspect. Defaults to Flask's
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.send_mail
documentation:
Send an email via the Flask-Mail extension.

```
param subject

Email subject

param recipient

Email recipient

param template

The name of the email template

param context

The context to render the template with
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.get_token_status
documentation:
Get the status of a token.

```
param token

The token to check

param serializer

The name of the seriailzer. Can be one of the
following: confirm, login, reset

param max_age

The name of the max age config option. Can be on of
the following: CONFIRM_EMAIL, LOGIN,
RESET_PASSWORD
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.use_double_hash
documentation: Return a bool indicating whether a password should be hashed twice.

functions.name: flask_security.utils.capture_registrations
documentation:
Testing utility for capturing registrations.

```
param confirmation_sent_at

An optional datetime object to set the
user's  to
```

functions.name: flask_security.utils.capture_reset_password_requests
documentation:
Testing utility for capturing password reset requests.

```
param reset_password_sent_at

An optional datetime object to set the
user's  to
```

```

The user to activate
```

documentation: Creates and returns a new role from the given parameters.

methods.name: flask_security.datastore.UserDatastore.find_or_create_role
documentation:
Returns a role matching the given name or creates it with any
additionally provided parameters.

methods.name: flask_security.datastore.UserDatastore.create_user
documentation: Creates and returns a new user from the given parameters.

methods.name: flask_security.datastore.UserDatastore.delete_user
documentation:
Deletes the specified user.

```
param user

The user to delete
```

Abstracted user datastore.

```
param user_model

A user model class definition

param role_model

A role model class definition
```

modules.name: flask_security.decorators
documentation:
flask_security.decorators
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Flask-Security decorators module

:copyright: (c) 2012 by Matt Wright.
:license: MIT, see LICENSE for more details.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: flask_security.decorators.auth_required
documentation:
Decorator that protects enpoints through multiple mechanisms
Example:

```
@app.route('/dashboard')
@auth_required('session')
def dashboard():
    return 'Dashboard'
```

```
param auth_methods

Specified mechanisms.
```

functions.name: flask_security.decorators.roles_required
documentation:
Decorator which specifies that a user must have all the specified roles.
Example:

```
@app.route('/dashboard')
@roles_required('admin', 'editor')
def dashboard():
    return 'Dashboard'
```

The current user must have both the `admin` role and `editor` role in order
to view the page.

```
param args

The required roles.
```

functions.name: flask_security.decorators.roles_accepted
documentation:
Decorator which specifies that a user must have at least one of the
specified roles. Example:

```
@app.route('/create_post')
@roles_accepted('editor', 'author')
def create_post():
    return 'Create Post'
```

The current user must have either the `editor` role or `author` role in
order to view the page.

```
param args

The possible roles.
```
